title: Lean Maturity Assessment
subtitle: Evaluate your factory across 6 core lean dimensions. 18 questions, about 5 minutes. Your results will reveal priority areas and recommended tools.
saveError: Failed to save assessment. Please try again.
recommendationsDesc: Categories are ranked from weakest to strongest. Focus on the top priorities first for maximum impact.
levelBeginnerDesc: Your factory has significant improvement opportunities. Start with foundational lean practices for quick wins.
levelDevelopingDesc: You are building lean foundations. The right tools will accelerate your journey significantly.
levelProficientDesc: Good progress! Focus on deepening practices and closing remaining gaps for consistent performance.
levelAdvancedDesc: Strong lean maturity. Sustain your gains and focus on advanced optimization and innovation.
catWorkplaceDesc: 5S/6S practices, visual workplace, and shop floor organization.
catVisualDesc: How well production performance is tracked, displayed, and communicated.
catStandardDesc: Documentation, adherence, and improvement of standard operating procedures.
catImprovementDesc: Culture of ongoing improvement, structured events, and employee involvement.
catProblemDesc: Root cause analysis methods, structured approaches, and data-driven decisions.
catTPMDesc: Equipment reliability, maintenance strategy, and operator involvement.
wp1Q: How organized is your shop floor?
wp1D1: Items are not labeled or organized. People frequently search for tools and materials. Clutter is common.
wp1D2: Some effort to keep things clean, but no formal system. Organization depends on individual habits.
wp1D3: 5S/6S standards exist in some areas. Some zones are well organized, others not yet covered.
wp1D4: 5S/6S is implemented everywhere with regular audits, scores tracked, and visual standards in place.
wp2D1: Workstations have no standard layout. Operators arrange things however they prefer.
wp2D2: A few workstations have been improved, but there is no systematic approach across the plant.
wp2D3: Most workstations follow ergonomic principles. Point-of-use storage is common for key tools.
wp2D4: All workstations are designed for minimal motion waste, with shadow boards, visual cues, and regular reviews.
wp3Q: How consistently are workplace standards maintained?
wp3D1: Even when things are cleaned up, standards degrade quickly. No one is responsible for sustaining order.
wp3D2: Supervisors sometimes check, but there is no routine or accountability system.
wp3D3: 5S/6S audits are done monthly or bi-weekly. Results are posted but corrective action is inconsistent.
wp3D4: Teams own their zones. Daily mini-audits, visible scores, and fast corrective action are the norm.
vm1Q: How is production performance displayed on the floor?
vm1D1: There are no boards, screens, or displays showing production status. Operators have no visibility.
vm1D2: Paper reports or whiteboards are updated at the end of each shift. Information is always delayed.
vm1D3: Target vs actual is tracked hourly on boards or screens. Gaps are visible within the shift.
vm1D4: Live dashboards show OEE, output, and alerts in real time. Anyone can see the current state instantly.
vm2Q: How quickly does leadership know about production problems?
vm2D1: Stoppages, quality issues, and delays are frequently discovered hours after they happen.
vm2D2: Verbal reports or phone calls reach supervisors eventually, but response is often too late.
vm2D3: Supervisors are notified promptly through structured communication or visual signals.
vm2D4: Digital andon systems or automated alerts notify the right people immediately when issues occur.
vm3Q: Can you see the flow of work from upstream to downstream?
vm3D1: It is hard to tell where work-in-progress is or whether the line is ahead or behind schedule.
vm3D2: Experienced operators know the general flow, but there is no visual system to show it.
vm3D3: Kanban cards, lane markers, or WIP limits exist in some areas but not consistently applied.
vm3D4: The entire value stream is visually managed with clear signals for pull, WIP limits, and flow status.
sw1Q: Are operating procedures documented and accessible?
sw1D1: Know-how lives in people's heads. If experienced operators leave, knowledge is lost.
sw1D2: A few procedures are written down, but they are outdated, incomplete, or hard to find.
sw1D3: Most critical processes have documented SOPs that operators can access at their workstations.
sw1D4: SOPs are current, visual, accessible, and regularly updated by the people who use them.
sw2Q: How well do operators follow standard work?
sw2D1: There is wide variation in how operators perform the same task. Quality is operator-dependent.
sw2D2: Standards exist but adherence is inconsistent. Deviations are common and not tracked.
sw2D3: Most operators follow documented methods. Supervisors occasionally verify adherence.
sw2D4: Standard work is followed consistently. Regular audits verify adherence and identify improvement opportunities.
sw3D1: Each operator does setups their own way. Changeover times are long and unpredictable.
sw3D2: Experienced operators share setup tips informally. There is no standard sequence.
sw3D3: Some changeover steps are documented with rough time estimates. Internal/external separation is beginning.
sw3D4: All changeovers follow a standard sequence with clear internal/external separation and targets.
ci1Q: Do operators and frontline workers suggest improvements?
ci1D1: There is no formal channel for improvement ideas. Workers feel their suggestions do not matter.
ci1D2: Some operators share ideas verbally, but there is no tracking or follow-up system.
ci1D3: A suggestion box or system is in place, but follow-up is slow and inconsistent.
ci1D4: Regular Kaizen events, ideas are tracked, implemented quickly, and contributors are recognized.
ci2Q: How well do you understand your value stream?
ci2D1: The flow of materials and information through the factory has never been formally analyzed.
ci2D2: People know the general process flow, but no documentation or analysis exists.
ci2D3: Flowcharts or process maps have been created, but value-add vs non-value-add analysis is limited.
ci2D4: Current and future state VSMs exist. Improvement actions are prioritized from the mapping exercise.
ci3Q: Does management regularly visit the shop floor to observe?
ci3D1: Management comes to the floor mainly to address complaints or escalations.
ci3D2: Managers walk the floor sometimes but without a structured approach or documented observations.
ci3D3: Scheduled floor visits happen, but observations are not systematically documented or followed up.
ci3D4: Gemba walks are scheduled, themed, documented, and result in visible improvement actions.
ps1Q: How do you investigate quality defects?
ps1D1: Defects are corrected immediately, but no analysis is done to find the root cause.
ps1D2: The supervisor identifies the cause based on experience, but without structured analysis.
ps1D3: Some informal investigation happens (asking around, checking recent changes), but not documented.
ps1D4: 5 Why, Ishikawa, or similar methods are used consistently. Root causes are documented and tracked.
ps2D1: Whichever customer complaint or issue is most urgent gets attention. No systematic prioritization.
ps2D2: The quality manager decides priorities based on experience, not data.
ps2D3: Defect data is collected but not regularly analyzed for patterns or Pareto prioritization.
ps2D4: Pareto analysis and trend data drive which problems get addressed first. Top issues are tracked to resolution.
ps3Q: How are complex, recurring problems handled?
ps3D1: Recurring problems are discussed in meetings but there is no structured problem-solving format.
ps3D2: Cross-functional meetings are held, but without a standard template or methodology.
ps3D3: A3 reports or PDCA cycles are used sometimes, but not consistently applied across the organization.
ps3D4: Complex problems follow a structured A3 or PDCA process with clear owners, timelines, and follow-up.
tp1D1: Equipment is fixed when it breaks. There is no planned maintenance schedule.
tp1D2: Some planned maintenance exists for critical equipment, but most work is still reactive.
tp1D3: Preventive maintenance schedules are in place for key equipment. Compliance is tracked.
tp1D4: A comprehensive TPM program exists with autonomous maintenance, planned maintenance, and focused improvement.
tp2Q: Do operators perform routine equipment care (CILT)?
tp2D1: Operators do not touch equipment maintenance. All work is done by the maintenance department.
tp2D2: Operators do some cleaning but no structured inspection, lubrication, or tightening tasks.
tp2D3: Basic CILT tasks are assigned to operators, but not standardized or consistently audited.
tp2D4: CILT routines are documented, operators are trained, tasks are scheduled, and compliance is audited.
tp3Q: How do you track equipment performance and reliability?
tp3D1: Equipment uptime, breakdowns, and MTBF are not measured. You only know something is wrong when it stops.
tp3D2: Some breakdown logs exist, but data is not analyzed for trends or improvement.
tp3D3: OEE or uptime is calculated for key equipment. Data is reviewed monthly.
tp3D4: OEE, MTBF, MTTR are tracked per machine. Data drives maintenance decisions and improvement priorities.
